segments.title: Introduction
segments.transcript: Welcome to this tutorial on React Hooks. Today we'll cover the most important hooks you need to know, starting with useState and useEffect, and then building our own custom hooks.
segments.speaker_notes: Welcome to this tutorial on React Hooks. Today we'll cover the most important hooks you need to know, starting with useState and useEffect, and then building our own custom hooks.

segments.title: What are Hooks?
segments.transcript: Hooks are functions that let you use state and other React features in functional components. Before hooks, you needed class components for state management. The key rules are: only call hooks at the top level, and only call hooks from React functions.
segments.speaker_notes: Hooks are functions that let you use state and other React features in functional components. Before hooks, you needed class components for state management. The key rules are: only call hooks at the top level, and only call hooks from React functions.

segments.title: useState Deep Dive
segments.transcript: Let's look at useState. You import it from React, then call it with an initial value. It returns an array with the current state and a setter function. const [count, setCount] = useState(0). You can use multiple useState calls for different pieces of state.
segments.speaker_notes: Let's look at useState. You import it from React, then call it with an initial value. It returns an array with the current state and a setter function. const [count, setCount] = useState(0). You can use multiple useState calls for different pieces of state.

segments.title: useEffect Patterns
segments.transcript: useEffect lets you perform side effects in your components. It runs after every render by default. You can control when it runs by passing a dependency array. An empty array means it only runs once on mount. Return a cleanup function to avoid memory leaks.
segments.speaker_notes: useEffect lets you perform side effects in your components. It runs after every render by default. You can control when it runs by passing a dependency array. An empty array means it only runs once on mount. Return a cleanup function to avoid memory leaks.

segments.title: Custom Hooks
segments.transcript: Custom hooks let you extract reusable logic. Name them with the use prefix. For example, function useWindowSize returns the current window dimensions. Custom hooks can call other hooks and compose nicely.
segments.speaker_notes: Custom hooks let you extract reusable logic. Name them with the use prefix. For example, function useWindowSize returns the current window dimensions. Custom hooks can call other hooks and compose nicely.

segments.title: Wrap Up
segments.transcript: That's it for today's tutorial on React Hooks. Remember: useState for state, useEffect for side effects, and custom hooks for reusable logic. Thanks for watching and see you next time.
segments.speaker_notes: That's it for today's tutorial on React Hooks. Remember: useState for state, useEffect for side effects, and custom hooks for reusable logic. Thanks for watching and see you next time.

slide_outline.title: What are Hooks?
bullets: Functions that let you use state and React features in functional components
Before hooks, class components were needed for state management
Only call hooks at the top level and from React functions

slide_outline.title: useState Deep Dive
bullets: Returns array with current state and setter
Use multiple useState for different state

slide_outline.title: useEffect Patterns
bullets: Dependency array controls when it runs
Empty array = run once on mount
Return cleanup function to avoid memory leaks

slide_outline.title: Custom Hooks
bullets: Extract reusable logic with use prefix
Can call other hooks and compose nicely

slide_outline.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: useEffect for side effects with dependency control
Custom hooks for reusable logic (use prefix)
Only call hooks at top level, from React functions
